data.title: Cogent Biosciences: Blockbuster Potential, Premium Valuation, Still A 'Buy'
data.description:
Cogent Overview

Cogent Biosciences, Inc. (COGT) has made a lot of progress since my last look in February 2023. My focus was on Cogent’s TKI, bezuclastinib, in gastrointestinal stromal tumors, or GIST. I believed that bezuclastinib and sunitinib was a promising combination in second-line GIST, pending Phase 3 (PEAK) confirmation.

Perhaps the biggest development during this time was not that bezuclastinib and sunitinib exceeded expectations in PEAK, but bezuclastinib’s newfound utility in nonadvanced systemic mastocytosis (NonAdvSM) and advanced systemic mastocytosis (AdvSM).

Although Sanofi’s (SNY) (formerly, Blueprint) TKI, Ayvakit, has a multi-year head start in this market, bezuclastinib’s potential should not be underappreciated.

Indeed, Cogent has its eyes on three separate approvals for bezuclastinib:

Below, I perform a sum-of-the-parts, or SOTP, valuation to inform whether or not COGT is still worth a Buy rating at these prices, as its stock has skyrocketed 169% since my last look.

GIST

There are roughly 6k cases of GIST in the U.S. per year.

Initial management includes imatinib, another TKI. About half of these patients will experience disease progression.

Sunitinib has been the SoC second-line agent for decades.

Cogent’s bezuclastinib did something special in PEAK that other TKIs (i.e., ripretinib) could not.

Combined with sunitinib, against sunitinib alone, it nearly doubled (79% improvement) mPFS.

In INTRIGUE, Deciphera gambled on ripretinib being a “cleaner” and “better” drug than sunitinib, but failed. Cogent’s “synergy” play paid off mightily.

So, although the U.S. Target Pop. is ~3,000 in 2L GIST, I can assume a robust 45% peak share.

Other assumptions include 12% WACC, 88% POS (NDA stage), 2027 Launch, 2031 Peak, 2038 LOE, 1.5% Pop. Growth, $380k Net Price, 20% Yr 1 Uptake, 55% Launch OpEx for 2 Yrs (first launch for company), 30% Steady-State OpEx, 8% COGS, and 2031 Competitor Entry Yr with 15% Competitive Impact (next-gen TKIs, combos).

I have bezuclastinib generating roughly $500M at peak in GIST, worth almost $1B in risk-adjusted NPV.

And should bezuclastinib become FDA-approved, its NPV climbs to $1.14B.

NonAdvSM

As I often point out when modeling drugs, prevalence does not equal TAM. This is especially true in NonAdvSM.

As I detailed in an earlier table, while Ayvakit is currently used in a group of patients, clinicians are hesitant to prescribe a drug with cognitive side effects for “non-cancerous” symptoms. So Ayvakit is typically reserved for refractory symptoms.

It’s possible that bezuclastinib’s more favorable side effect profile could expand the TAM.

When it comes to modeling bezuclastinib in NonAdvSM, I have to account for Sanofi/Blueprint existing competition (Ayvakit) and future competition (Sanofi's/Blueprint's next-gen BLU-263 in Phase 2/3).

My assumptions for bezuclastinib in NonAdvSM include 12% WACC, 88% POS, 2027 Launch, 2031 Peak, 2040 LOE, 10k Target Pop., 2.5% Pop. Growth (expanding TAM d/t better diagnosis and more tolerable TKIs), 20% Peak Share, $380k Net Price, 10% Yr 1 Uptake, 55% Launch OpEx for 2 Yrs, 30% Steady-State OpEx, 8% COGS, and 2029 Competitor Entry Yr with 25% Competitive Impact.

So, for NonAdvSM, I have bezuclastinib generating roughly $750M at peak, good for $1.37B in total rNPV.

AdvSM

Sanofi’s/Blueprint’s Ayvakit is also used in AdvSM. But, again, bezuclastinib appears differentiated.

The funnel for AdvSM is tighter than the indolent form because of its severity. So, unlike NonAdvSM, many patients with AdvSM are candidates for immediate TKI therapy.

Modeling assumptions for bezuclastinib in AdvSM include 3k Target Pop., 12% WACC, 88% POS, 2028 Launch, 2031 Peak, 2041 LOE, 2% Pop. Growth, 20% Peak Share, $380k Net Price, 10% Yr 1 Uptake, 45% Launch OpEx (lower because Cogent will already have much of the infrastructure in place from GIST and NonAdvSM launches), 30% Steady-State OpEx, and 2031 Competitive Entry Yr with 12% Competitive Impact.

Smaller market. ~$250M (accounting for Pop. Growth) in peak sales. Roughly $400M in rNPV.

Corporate Assumptions & Pipeline SOTP

Putting it all together, COGT appears a bit overvalued at current prices.

COGT’s "Intrinsic Equity Value" comes out to $4.3B, or 23.7% below COGT's "Current Market Cap." Intrinsic Equity Value is its pipeline (U.S. and Global) and cash, minus debt, divided by Shares Out (161M).

To calculate "ROW Value," I multiplied the "U.S. Pipeline Base" by "0.4." This is a rule of thumb. It's purposely simplistic, lest I spend days modeling Cogent's pipeline in several different ex-U.S. markets that I am less familiar with.

The Fair Value/Share of $26.71 is derived from simply dividing the Intrinsic Equity Value by COGT's Shares Out (161M).

I did model that Cogent’s $901M in cash and investments is sufficient for Cogent to reach a cash-generative state.

Management estimates (Slide 36) that its cash runway extends “through U.S. commercial launch into 2028.” So, I wouldn’t completely rule out more dilution.

Now, Cogent outright owns bezuclastinib. I imagine they could easily secure non-dilutive funding in the form of a global partnership. This is actually a part of management’s corporate goals in 2026 (look at Slide 36 again).

Because I see COGT as a takeover candidate, I included a “M&A Overlay” in the SOTP image.

When I model, I purposely lean conservative in my estimates, so I’m not too concerned about differences between “Fair Value/Share” and “Current Share Price.”

Also, I just modeled bezuclastinib. Cogent’s pipeline includes a few other assets. Although, because they are all in pre-clinical/early clinical stages, it’s not unreasonable to assume they are effectively worth zero rNPV. If anything, given the nature of drug development (high R&D costs, low POS, etc.), they could be valued less than that.

Conclusion

Bezuclastinib is primed to become a blockbuster in GIST and SM, and I suspect that it is garnering a lot of interest from potential partners/acquirers. This likely explains COGT’s apparent premium valuation.

COGT's premium valuation also may account for its 9.69% Short Interest. NDA events are increasingly uncertain and being a “second-comer, next-gen TKI” could make for a challenging and costly launch.

From my desk, so long as Cogent continues to de-risk bezuclastinib and, assuming it expands its TAMs, I’m willing to maintain a Buy rating on its stock despite obvious competitive, regulatory, and valuation risks.

data.title: Dollar Index Rally Runs Out Of Steam- UDN Is A Bearish Dollar Index ETF
data.description:
The dollar index measures the U.S. dollar’s value against other leading reserve currencies, with the euro being the most exposed.

The chart shows that the euro accounts for 57.6% of the dollar index’s value. The dollar index has been in a bearish trend since reaching 114.78 in Q3 2022, falling to a low of 95.55 in Q1 2026. However, the war in Iran has caused the dollar index to recover to around 100.

While the U.S. dollar remains the world’s leading reserve fiat currency, gold has overtaken the dollar as the choice asset for central bank reserves.

A January 30, 2026, article on Statista stated:

For the first time since 1996, foreign central banks now hold more gold than U.S. Treasuries (U.S. debt) in their reserves, marking a historic turning point in global reserve management. Gold holdings overtaking U.S. Treasuries signals a rebalancing of global reserves, with implications for the dollar’s global dominance.

While the impact on the dollar index is not necessarily bearish, as all of the other currencies that compose the index have lost value, de-dollarization and the bearish trend could mean the index will continue to make lower lows. The Invesco DB U.S. Dollar Index Bearish Fund (UDN) moves higher as the dollar index declines.

The dollar index recovers

After reaching a low of 95.55 on January 27, 2026, the dollar index turned higher.

The daily chart shows that the dollar index has rallied 5.33%, moving from the January 27, 2026, low of 95.55 to the March 31, 2026, high of 100.64.

Consolidation, but the bearish trend remains intact

While the dollar index has rallied over the past two months, the long-term trend since the Q3 2022 high and since the 2001 and 1985 peaks remains bearish.

The quarterly chart since the 1960s shows that the dollar index has trended lower since the Q3 2022 high of 114.78, reaching 95.55 in late January, the lowest level since Q1 2022.

Meanwhile, the dollar index has made lower highs since Q3 2001, when it reached 121.02, and Q1 1985, when it peaked at 164.72. While the dollar index has recovered from its most recent early 2026 low, the long-term bearish trend remains firmly intact.

Debt, wars, and political division weigh on the dollar index

The dollar index reached its most recent low before the U.S. attack on Iran. Since the dollar remains the leading reserve fiat currency, the hostilities in the Middle East boosted the dollar index. The U.S. currency typically attracts buying during times of geopolitical turmoil.

Meanwhile, the U.S. debt, which is over $39 trillion, is a bearish noose around the U.S. currency’s neck. Even if spending and receipts offset, the debt level rises by over $1.4 trillion each year with the Fed Funds Rate at 3.625%. The rising debt weighed on the dollar’s value.

Political division in the U.S. going into the midterm elections could derail the administration’s policies if Democrats capture majorities in the House of Representatives and/or the Senate. The bottom line is that while the geopolitical landscape has lifted the dollar index from its most recent low, debt and domestic political division remain bearish factors. Meanwhile, the bifurcation of the world’s nuclear powers that has led to sanctions and U.S. tariffs on trading partners has increased a trend toward global de-dollarization, which is also bearish for the U.S. currency and the dollar index.

Critical technical support is not far away

While the long-term U.S. dollar index chart dating back to the 1960s remains bearish, the 20-Year monthly chart displays a completely different picture.

Interestingly, the 20-Year dollar index chart displays a bullish pattern of higher lows and higher highs. On the 20-Year chart, technical support is at the recent low of 95.55. Below there, the January 2021 low of 89.20 is the critical support level.

On the upside, technical resistance is at the January 2025 high of 110.17 and the September 2022 high of 114.78. At the 100 level, the dollar index is closer to support than resistance.

Debt, sanctions, tariffs, and the upcoming midterm elections could favor a decline in the dollar index, while the geopolitical landscape remains bullish, as indicated by recent price action. I favor the downside in the dollar index as all fiat currencies have lost value, and the trend toward de-dollarization continues.

UDN is a bearish dollar index ETF

The Invesco DB U.S. Dollar Index Bearish Fund ETF appreciates as the dollar index declines. At $18.02 per share in early April, UDN had $143.53 million in assets under management. UDN trades an average of over 267,000 shares per day, making it a liquid ETF. The ETF charges a 0.75% management fee. U.S. interest rate differentials across the dollar index components yield $0.54, roughly a 3% yield.

The most recent decline in the dollar index took it 13.27% lower from 110.17 in January 2025 to the January 2026 low of 95.55.

The chart shows that over the same period, the UDN ETF rose 13.83% from $16.49 to $18.77 per share.

The recent recovery in the dollar index results in a D grade in Seeking Alpha’s ETF Grade momentum category. The 0.75% management fee receives a D+ in the expense category, while the 3% yield earns a B- in the dividend category. The assets under management and daily trading volume earn a B- in liquidity, while the currency markets receive a D+ grade in the risk category.

During times when the U.S. stock market is closed, UDN can miss highs or lows in the dollar index, which trades around the clock.

While the dollar index has rallied from the January low, debt, the trend toward de-dollarization, and the risks of the upcoming midterm election could mean it will run out of upward momentum around the 100 level. I rate UDN a buy as the odds favor a continuation of lower lows in the dollar index.

data.title: Adeia: How The Company Is Turning Into The Secret Engine Of HBM Chips
data.description:
Adeia Inc (ADEA) is traditionally known as an old patent company in the pay-TV sector. However, it decided to transform into a key player in the semiconductor industry, as its portfolio covers some areas, including hybrid bonding, which is necessary to develop the next generation of HBM memory chips.

However, the market still didn't grasp this significant transformation, which could change the company's trajectory, given its recent agreement with AMD and the renewal of its strategic partnership with Taiwanese UMC, in addition to its recent deals with Disney and Microsoft, which allow them to access ADEA's media portfolio. This would offset ADEA's decline in the pay TV sector and simultaneously provide it with strong catalysts that could change the market view of the stock and consider re-rating it.

The company owns a large portfolio of around 14,000 patents that are distributed across two major sectors, media and semiconductors. The company's business model relies on collecting licensing fees from global companies that use its patents. The beauty of this model is that it has a high operating leverage, so once the company covers its patent costs, including R&D and legal fees, any additional dollar it generates from new licenses is directly converted into net profit, which explains the reason behind the company's high profit margins compared to traditional manufacturing companies.

Management realized that the traditional cable TV segment is becoming obsolete, so it's gradually shifting this part of business to over-the-top "OTT" companies and uses the cash flow from the traditional sector to fund its innovations in semiconductor, as the future is there.

Hybrid-Bonding technology

For decades, the semiconductor industry has been relying on Moore's Law, as the number of transistors doubles every two years. A large chip was manufactured to include the CPU, GPU, and even memory, but after reaching 3- and 2-nanometer chips, the chip manufacturing cost became very high. The industry saw that the solution is to cut the chip into smaller pieces called Chiplets. For example, a piece of the CPU, another for the GPU, and so on. They then combine these pieces together like LEGO bricks.

The Engineers are currently using very fine solder bumps to connect these small chips. However, these bumps take up a significant area of the chip and generate high heat that reduces the data transfer speeds.Adeia realizes that the solution is in binding the chips together using molecules oxide to oxide and copper to copper.This allows the data to flow faster in a smaller area with efficient energy and improvement in the heat. The industry report indicates that the manufacturers may continue to use the solder bumps in the first generation of HBM chips "HBM4" to save costs and reduce risks, so they will likely adopt the new technology in the next generation HBM4E by 2027.

Financial Results & Outlook

The company achieved exceptional results during 2025 as it recorded revenues of $182.6 million in Q4 alone, with an increase of 109% compared to Q3 and 53% YoY. Adeia also secured 9 major deals during this quarter, including 4 new clients such as Disney, in addition to settling some catch-up payments.

Additionally, the company has tremendous operating leverage, as adjusted EBITDA achieved 62.6%, which is equivalent to $278 million of the total revenues, which means that any increase in the revenues should be recorded as a net profit.

The company generated cash flows of $158 million during the year as it paid $60 million for debts to reduce future interest expenses and purchased $20 million worth of shares, increasing the EPS and the equity of existing shareholders.

The company anticipates achieving an average adjusted EBITDA of $213-245 million, which represents a strong operating margin of 55%. It also expects revenues of $415 million, which is lower than the $443 million in 2025, as the last year included substantial one-time payments and some settlements with companies like Disney. Therefore, 2026 revenues should be more recurring with higher quality.

However, the company didn't provide specific guidance for Q1, but the management noted that the first and second halves of the year will have equal revenues. Therefore, we can assume that the revenues of the first half will be around $207 million, the revenues for Q1 could range between $100 and $105 million, which represents a normal and seasonal decrease from Q4, but reflects a strong start for the year driven by Microsoft licensing revenues.

Value Per Share

Given that the company operates in different sectors, we will value each sector separately based on its percentage of 2026 guidance. Then we will combine them to calculate the enterprise value.

The media sector represents around 94% of the company's total revenues. InterDigital the closest company with gross margin +80% trades at EV/EBITDA multiple of 17x. Assuming the segment will generate $210 million with multiple of 14x, the value of this segment would be around $2.94 billion.

The semiconductor represents approximately 6% of Adeia's revenues. While similar companies are trading at multiples of 20-50x due to their vital role in the AI industry, Adeia offers a profit margin of nearly 90% without needing to spend on factories or equipment. Therefore, I will give it a multiple of 30x as this the key driver which grows at %40 annually. Assuming this sector is going to generate $20 million in EBITDA, the value of this sector should be $600 million.

If we combine the two sectors together to get the EV for the company, we will get approximately $3.54 billion. Subtracting net debt of 263 million. we can conclude that the equity value is around $3.3 billion or $29 per share.

However, as the company continues to move toward the semiconductor sector, and getting closer to $100 million recurring revenues with adopting the next generation HBM4E chips, the valuation would easily getting higher.

Risks To Consider

Adeia operates primarily as a licensing firm, so the business relies on its ability to convince other companies to pay. If a tech company decides to use Adeia's patent without an agreement, Adeia will resort to court for compensation. However, litigation costs millions of dollars and probably takes years. Furthermore, if Adeia loses a case, this would invalidate its patent forever, thus losing a portion of its future revenue. Nevertheless, the company has experience in the courts, and has already filed cases against major companies, such as its litigation against AMD regarding hybrid bonding technology. Ultimately, AMD opted for a settlement and signed a licensing agreement with it a few weeks ago rather than risking a court ruling.

The company also understands that patents aren't perpetual and have an expiration date. Therefore, it makes tactical acquisitions to support its portfolio, as in 2025 alone, it made six small acquisitions to secure its portfolio and ensure continued legal protection.

Regarding Cable TV, the company expects a decline to 35% of total revenues. Therefore, there is a risk that this sector erodes faster than the company's ability to compensate for it through semiconductor growth, so the company significantly invested in the OTT segment and signed agreements with Disney and Microsoft to mitigate this decline in the TV market.

Final Thoughts

I think Adeia is on the verge of a major transformation, either in terms of replacing its traditional TV business with licensing agreements in the OTT sector, or its innovations in the semiconductor industry. In the next few quarters, we should monitor any announcements of new deals with top-tier memory manufacturers such as Micron or Samsung, which will be a strong catalyst for the stock. Additionally, it's crucial to ensure that the OTT revenue is moving toward exceeding the 30% mark of total revenue to mitigate the risks associated with the company's image as a traditional licensing provider.

data.title: The Empty Tank: Why I Am Shorting Avis Budget
data.description:
In the rental car trade, the model is simple: buy, rent, and sell. But when those "moving parts" grind against $28 billion in debt, the music stops. Avis (CAR) recently announced a 5-million-share offering (5 million share offering) a jarring U-turn for a company that spent years aggressively buying back its own stock. This is a clear admission that the "cannibal" has run out of appetite and now requires a public life raft.

The Arithmetic of Desperation

The wisdom of a share sale depends on the price. Selling when overvalued is genius; selling when undervalued is a discount on your future. With a $6 billion market cap, this offering is a massive "ask." In my opinion, management is signaling that survival outweighs concentration, diluting the "equity stub" to prevent their debt mountain from becoming a tombstone. This is a klaxon of structural necessity, not a sign of strength.

The $34 Billion Elephant

Avis carries $28 billion in net debt, giving it an Enterprise Value (EV) of roughly $34 billion. The business is essentially a massive pile of debt with a tiny sliver of equity sitting on top.

Based on the most recent data (Avis latest 10-K) , the debt stack is effectively split into two distinct engines of risk:

Vehicle Program Debt (~$19.3 Billion): This fleet-backed leverage is secured by asset-backed securities (ABS) and revolving lines, meaning the cars themselves are the collateral.

Corporate Debt (~$6.0 Billion): This "unsecured" layer consists of senior notes and term loans, representing a direct claim on the company’s dwindling cash flow rather than specific physical assets. The issuance of the new 5 million shares could extinguish about 10/15% of this debt pile.

The math is increasingly grim: interest expenses have ballooned to over 11% of revenues, and debt servicing has now surpassed $1 billion annually. This is a critical threshold. During the average of the past three years, $1 billion represented the lion's share of the company's EBITDA. When your interest payments begin to devour your entire cash-generating power, you are no longer driving the car—the debt is.

A Decade of Running in Place

A 10-year lookback reveals a Revenue CAGR of 3.20%, while EBITDA growth was a ghostly 0.04%. For a decade, Avis has managed a larger fleet and served more customers without gaining a single horsepower in actual profit. Despite analyst hopes for a 2027 recovery, the immediate reality is a "valuation gap." Paying 3.00x EV/Sales—up from 2.25x three years ago—for a stagnant commodity business taxed by 11% interest on their revenue is a triumph of hope over experience.

The Hazards for Short Sellers

Debt is a double-edged sword. While the leveraged balance sheet is a risk—recalling the challenges that have humbled even the most astute allocators in this sector—it also provides a "slingshot" effect. If the wind catches the sails, leverage can catapult returns at a rate far exceeding actual business growth.

The catalysts for such a move may be approaching:

  • The Tariff Wall: A global tariff war could spike the price of imported vehicles. Because Avis already owns its "inventory," rising new car prices act as a rising tide for used car values, turning their fleet into a "golden goose."
  • The Travel Shift: Turmoil in U.S. air travel may drive travelers toward the certainty of the rental counter.
  • The Short Squeeze: With 20% short interest, any positive momentum could trigger a violent "squeeze," as we saw in the post-pandemic era.

The Verdict: A Structural Short in a Sea of Debt

In the world of value investing, we look for businesses that produce more than they consume. Avis Budget Group has become the inverse: a capital-devouring machine where the owners are now being asked to subsidize a $28 billion mountain of debt.

I am moving to a Short position on CAR for four definitive reasons:

  1. The Interest Trap: Debt servicing has now crossed the $1 billion annual mark, effectively consuming almost the entirety of the company's average three-year EBITDA. When a business works purely to pay its lenders, shareholders are left with the crumbs of a stagnant table.
  2. The Strategic Flip-Flop: The management’s erratic pivot from an aggressive "EV future" to a massive $518 million impairment charge (Avis 10-K) and a retreat back to internal combustion shows a lack of navigational clarity. This "correction" is not a one-time event; it is a signal of a fleet strategy that is reacting to the market rather than leading it.
  3. Dilution as a Survival Tactic: The 5 million share offering is a white flag. After years of aggressive buybacks, management is now selling equity into a stagnant market to provide a "cushion" for their leverage. This is not "growth capital"—it is a life jacket for a balance sheet that is starting to sink.
  4. The Stagnation of the Commodity: With a 10-year profit growth rate of effectively zero, Avis has proven it cannot scale its way out of its obligations. It remains a commodity business in a high-interest world, forced to run faster just to stay in the same place.

While the 20% short interest creates the risk of a "slingshot" rally, the fundamental gravity of $1 billion in annual interest and a flat-lining bottom line makes the long-term trajectory clear. The road for Avis is no longer open—it is a steep, uphill climb with an empty tank. I prefer to bet on the descent.

data.title: Delta Air Q1 Earnings Preview: A High Bar To Fly Over, Shares Fairly Valued
data.description:
When Delta Air Lines (DAL) reports its Q1 on April 8, investor attention will likely be captivated by two recent headlines. Rising fuel costs and shutdown-related airport strains.

In its business update in mid-March, DAL reported that it is positioned strongly to navigate the current unfavorable environment. It even raised its revenue guidance for the upcoming quarter. I’ll be watching results to see if DAL can live up to its raised bar.

So far, investors like what they see. Shares are outperforming the sector and are still performing fairly well YTD despite the unfavorable operating environment.

There appears to be consensus bullishness on DAL across the analyst communities. However, I am taking a more "Neutral" view, and I see shares fairly valued ahead of its Q1 release.

DAL Stock Key Metrics

At about 10x forward earnings, I view DAL as fairly priced, both to historical averages and relative to the peer set. The stock’s recent outperformance has also earned DAL a slight premium to the group.

I view DAL as reasonably priced in other measures as well. This includes its enterprise value to forward EBITDA, which currently sits around 6.5x. That’s right in line with where I would expect it to trade.

Though the valuation appears adequately priced on the surface, the vast majority of analysts covering the stock see further room for DAL to run.

On Wall Street, for example, analysts see shares fairly valued at around the $80/share mark. This would represent nearly 20% upside potential from current trading levels.

Sentiment is similarly bullish within the Seeking Alpha (“SA”) analyst community. I believe DAL does have a few catalysts to get here, including rising consumer and corporate demand fundamentals. However, I do believe DAL could have a high bar to fly over with its increased guidance.

What Is The Outlook For DAL Stock?

In my view, DAL already had positive momentum coming out of Q4 and entering 2026.

Initial guidance provided in Q4 forecasted topline growth of 5% to 7%. This was supported by positive unit revenue, improving corporate demand, and continued strength in premium and loyalty streams.

At the time, I already saw that as a positive, given the tough operating environment the industry was facing.

But the more important update came in mid-March.

At the JPMorgan conference, DAL raised that Q1 revenue outlook, citing demand trends running several points above the original 5%–7% range. CEO Ed Bastian highlighted that DAL recorded 8 of the 10 highest sales days in its history during the quarter, with bookings up roughly 25% YOY in the week of the presentation.

Furthermore, the strength was broad-based across corporate, international, premium leisure, and domestic demand.

To me, the guidance hike is moderately surprising, especially given the near doubling in jet fuel prices, winter weather disruptions that cut capacity by about two points, and geopolitical uncertainty abroad.

That tells me DAL’s revenue engine, which includes premium seating, loyalty, co-brand spend, and brand pricing power, is working exactly as designed.

What To Watch When DAL Reports Q1?

Heading into the print, I’ll be tuned into two primary operational themes. This will be in addition to DAL’s top and bottom line performance making the headlines.

First, I’ll be listening closely for commentary around airport congestion tied to the recent TSA staffing strains during the government shutdown. While long security lines and passenger bottlenecks don’t show up directly in revenue metrics, they absolutely affect customer experience, on-time performance, and operational costs.

DAL has long prided itself on reliability and a premium travel experience, so any updated discussion around how these disruptions impacted load factors, cancellations, or customer satisfaction will be important context for the quarter, in my view.

Second, I expect at least a brief mention of DAL’s newly announced partnership with Amazon (AMZN) to power next-generation in-flight Wi-Fi through its LEO satellite network.

For me, this isn’t a near-term needle mover. Rollout doesn’t begin until 2028, after all. But it does support DAL’s ongoing investment in the customer experience and its digital ecosystem. The tie-in with AMZN Web Services also hints at deeper personalization and loyalty integration down the road.

I don’t think either of these items will drive Q1 results, but both fit into DAL’s broader narrative, nonetheless. That is, protecting the premium brand while continuing to invest in the long-term travel experience.

Is DAL Stock A Buy, Sell, Or Hold?

Despite the positive demand trends and raised guidance, I view DAL shares as a "Hold" heading into the Q1 print.

DAL is performing well operationally, in my view. The revenue engine is firing across premium, corporate, loyalty, and international segments, even in the face of rising fuel prices, winter weather disruptions, and geopolitical uncertainty. That’s a testament to how differentiated the business model has become over the past decade.

I do believe, however, that much of this strength already appears reflected in the stock. Shares have outperformed peers and now trade at a slight premium to the group on forward earnings and EBITDA metrics.

At roughly 10x forward earnings and 6.5x EV/EBITDA, the valuation looks fair relative to both history and the current operating environment.

I also think that DAL has set a high bar for itself. By raising Q1 guidance and highlighting record sales trends, expectations are elevated. I believe any signs of margin pressure from fuel, operational strains from airport congestion, or softer commentary around forward bookings could temper investor enthusiasm.

I still see long-term catalysts in DAL's premium strategy, loyalty ecosystem, and brand strength. But for now, I’m content staying on the sidelines and watching whether DAL can clear the higher bar it has created.

data.title: Crude Oil Structure Offers Interesting ETF Trading Opportunities
data.description:
We have previously covered the term structure in crude oil and focused on how backwardation and contango and dramatically impact your returns. We have an unusual setup once more and want to focus on how investors could play it in light of the geopolitical situation.

The Structure

Unlike when you buy a stock, with commodity futures you always buy (or sell) for an underlying delivery date. In the case of crude oil, we are currently in the most intense backwardation the world has ever seen. What we mean by that is the futures for December 2028 are $33 below the front month.

This is an interesting development as it allows investors to make more than the standard "buy oil cause it is going up" bets. Let's look at how that works out.

United States Oil Fund LP ETF (USO)

USO is the most famous fund in the group an has been around for about two decades. USO primarily holds the May futures, with about one fifth of its assets in the June futures. Both these pertain to West Texas Intermediate traded on NYMEX.

USO will slowly "roll" its May futures into June. So everyday it will sell a little of its May futures as the expiration comes up, and buy some June futures. Similarly, it will roll its June to the July futures. This roll is generally a costly process for the ETF when the commodity market is in its normal state of slight contango. For example, if May 2026 futures were at $110 and the June Futures were at $110.50 (slight contango), every roll costs the fund a $0.50 to hold the same number of barrels. But today the roll is highly profitable. It creates an extremely profitable situation. When the fund sells the May futures and buys the June futures, it nets more than $11, while holding the same number of barrels.

What this means for your trading is that if oil is trading at $110 in December 2026, USO will be up big. A good way to visualize this is to assume if the transaction happened today. USO would sell its May and June futures for December and land up with far more barrels of oil. Then those barrels would rise in price and catch up to the current $110 price. So if your outlook is that oil trades at or over $110 in December 2026, USO is an excellent way to play it, based on the current structure.

UBS ETRACS Crude Oil Shares Covered Call ETN (USOI)

We have previously written about this specific ETF and you can see our last article here. USOI sells covered calls on crude oil futures and while that can be a good thing, we hate "blind" or "mechanical" covered call selling. In this case, you can lose a lot of upside and these rolls become more problematic. Anyone doubting that can see the performance over the last month.

That said, if you expect a flattish price, with each month slowly catching up to the current futures, USOI should do well.

Invesco DB Energy Fund (DBE)

This is a very interesting fund that is rarely covered on Seeking Alpha. It holds energy. It holds 6 different types of energy futures including Brent Oil, Natural Gas and Gasoline. What is even more interesting is that doesn't blindly roll futures. It will find the roll that gives it the most bang for the buck and roll there. So if it sees the next month futures are trading $1 higher but the 12 month out futures are $10 lower, it will buy those. You can see that this results in it holding very different expiries across its products.

You can see that in the last month DBE has underperformed USO, but far less than USOI.

What is more interesting is that DBE's diversification and roll strategies have protected it during the COVID-19 downturn a lot better and it has really walloped USO.

DBE offers one of the best places to go if you are bullish but want someone to think before they roll.

Outlook & Other Strategies

Extreme Contango, the kind we have now, is usually when oil prices peak. Tom McClellan (as shared on his open website), has shown that front month exceeding the 12 month out futures by even $10, is usually close to the peak in crude oil futures.

Currently we are at Russia invasion levels. Our own research was showing a massive glut, before the war and we think those fundamentals will likely reassert. Even the market is not completely in the dark about it. Despite all this bullish news for crude oil, the 2028 strip is trading well under $70.

If you are madly bullish and want to express that view with a defined risk-reward, you could consider buying a call option on the longer dated futures. These are surprisingly cheap as the volatility of those futures has been minimal. So the December 2028 $100 strikes are trading for just $2.00 a barrel.

If you believe in a permanent change in pricing (we don't), that's the sweet bet. Similarly, the December 2028, $70 calls are trading for just $8.00. Keep in mind these are quite illiquid and you need to use patient limit orders. For our money, we currently don't have any positions. We had recently shorted Texas Pacific Land (TPL) at $502, but covered it yesterday. Energy stocks, especially oil stocks, remain extremely expensive in our view relative to longer term prospects. We are monitoring the situation for now and might trade if the setup looks good.

Please note that this is not financial advice. It may seem like it, sound like it, but surprisingly, it is not. Investors are expected to do their own due diligence and consult a professional who knows their objectives and constraints. Most of our articles are not written for "buy and hold" investors.

data.title: TD Synnex: Expecting EPS Growth To Remain Very Strong
data.description:
Investment overview

I wrote about TD Synnex Corp. (SNX) previously with a buy rating as growth continues to come through, margins remain healthy, and I expect earnings to continue growing, which would drive a positive multiple re-rating. After looking at the Q1 results, I still think SNX is a buy. Hyve is scaling faster than I expected and becoming a much larger profit driver, while the core distribution business is also still growing well. At the same time, margins continue to improve, and working capital stayed under control.

1Q26 earnings review

SNX started 2026 with a very strong quarter. Revenue was up 18% y/y, coming in at $17.16 billion, and adj. gross billings at $25.78 billion, up 24% y/y. The growth was broad-based. Distribution revenue saw $15.01 billion, up 17% y/y. Within that, Endpoint Solutions revenue saw $8.51 billion, up 19% y/y, and Advanced Solutions revenue saw $6.5 billion, up 15% y/y. Hyve Solutions revenue came in strong at $2.15 billion, up 24% y/y. Margins and profits did really well too. For gross profit, SNX saw $1.25 billion, and gross margin was 7.3%, up 32 bps y/y; adj., EBIT saw $590 million, and adj. EBIT margin reached 3.44%, up 60 bps y/y, and at the bottom line, adj. net income came in at $383 million, and adj. EPS at $4.73.

Hyve is becoming much more important

If you have followed my update on SNX, you would know that Hyve is a key part of the story. The growth was already strong, but I now believe the growth strength is a lot stronger than I thought. Gross billings here effectively grew a near 100% y/y, which is significantly faster than the 17% gross billings growth in Distribution. Importantly, Hyve EBIT also grew 66% y/y, and this now accounts for 27% of total EBIT. This dramatically shifts the growth story for SNX. If 27% of total EBIT can grow at 66%, the contribution from Hyve alone is ~18% to blended EBIT. If anything, this quarter cemented the narrative that Hyve is the key growth driver for the coming years.

What makes this more compelling is that the customer base appears to be widening, which means growth could potentially accelerate. In the call, management said Hyve has onboarded 3 new US hyperscaler customers and also said that these programs are significant in size. It was also noted those newer programs are expected to ramp more meaningfully toward the end of FY26 and into FY27. Firstly, this should ease the concern that Hyve has concentration risk. Secondly, this implies that the growth we see today is not the peak yet.

Margin expansion

I also think the margin expansion story deserves attention. The adj. EBIT margin expansion of ~60bps y/y was supported by expansion across all segments. Distribution gross margin expanded ~30bps y/y, and Hyve gross margin expanded ~150bps y/y. By my math, the blended incremental adj. EBITDA margin (using adj. EBITDA margin for better consistency) is trending at high single digits, and the current adj. EBITDA margin is mid-single digits. This means there is still room for margin to expand by a significant amount. We also know that Hyve is growing much faster than the rest of the business and carries a higher margin, and that tells me the current P&L is not really accurately reflecting the margin potential here. For what it's worth, CDW Corp. (CDW) has an adj. EBITDA margin of ~9%, so I don’t think my analysis is a far-fetched idea.

Balance sheet

Lastly, I would like to touch on the balance sheet. I have given my thoughts on this previously (believing that SNX has turned this into a positive situation), and I think that still stays true. While Q1 2026 cash from operations was -$896 million and free cash flow [FCF] was -$929 million, which on the surface looks like a sharp reversal from the very strong Q4 cash flow print, I do not think that should be the focus. What matters more, I believe, is whether SNX was able to have good control of its working capital, because cash flow in this business can swing sharply quarter to quarter depending on when SNX builds inventory, collects receivables, and pays suppliers, especially when Hyve is growing quickly, and I do think they did really well. Inventory did rise, which shouldn’t be a surprise given how fast some parts of the business are growing, especially Hyve.

But when I look at the key operating metrics, I don’t think this is really much of a concern. Days payables outstanding went up slightly to 102 days from 87 days last year; days inventory went up a bit to 62 from 56, and days sales outstanding was also up slightly to 63 from 58. All in all, we actually saw the cash conversion cycle go down by 4 days, to 23 days from 27 days in Q1 2025. In other words, the quarter did use more cash, but SNX was able to recycle working capital faster. This should serve as another direct counterpoint to the bear's thoughts that Hyve growth would eat up working capital and start stressing the balance sheet.

Valuation

I still see attractive upside to SNX’s shares. There are two key changes to my model: (1) FY26 adj. EPS to grow by 30%. I believe this is possible given the Q1 strong performance and management Q2 guide for $4 in adjusted EPS. If we extrapolate this H1 performance to H2, $17 adjusted. EPS doesn’t seem like a stretch. For FY27/28, I stuck to my previous assumption, but note that it could be stronger than expected, like in FY26. The strong adj. EPS growth should also drive up multiples higher than I thought. I now model at 13x NTM PE vs. 12.5x NTM PE previously. Together, I raise my price target to $277.

Risk

While Hyve’s customer base is getting wider, it is still becoming a much larger part of SNX’s profit pool, so SNX’s equity story is bound to get more swings if growth from the current hyperscaler base slows or if newer customer programs ramp more slowly than expected. I would also continue to pay attention to working capital. The truth is Hyve still requires inventory to be funded before cash comes back, so if growth stays fast but turns lumpier, cash conversion could remain volatile and weigh on sentiment even if earnings stay strong.

Conclusion

I reiterate my buy rating on SNX. Hyve is becoming a more important earnings driver, the core business remains healthy, and margins should continue to expand. The balance sheet also looks better than the headline suggests once we dive deeper into the working capital mechanics.

data.title: Why I'm Buying Applied Optoelectronics, Despite Its Dark History
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Last month, I issued a detailed post on why I advised investors to be bullish on Applied Optoelectronics, Inc. (AAOI) but be extremely watchful about any gaps in the execution of Applied Optoelectronics or AOI’s strategic capacity expansion plans.

I explained reasons for my cautiously bullish outlook in last month’s post, noting how AOI had enough precedents from the past to be cautious despite the raging demand for optical networking products such as the transceivers and small-form factor pluggables that AOI makes for the data center market.

Through this month, AOI’s management has continued to announce major hyperscaler customer program wins, which carry the potential to add at least $253 million to AOI’s revenue prospects this year. This not only adds more firepower to AOI’s growth engine but broadens out the customer concentration risk for AOI that I had highlighted in my March post.

I believe these contract wins were enough for me to start dipping my toes and take my bullishness on AOI to the next level by initiating some exposure in AOI.

What AOI’s Recent Hyperscaler Wins Mean For AOI

In the last few weeks, AOI announced that it had won two major program wins for its optical transceiver business, where it would provide both 800Gbps and 1.6Tbps transceivers to the same hyperscaler customer.

At the start of March, AOI revealed that this 1.6T program win was just an “initial order," which totaled ~$200M from “one of its long-term major hyperscale customers.” There were two implications from this announcement.

First, all shipments of this 1.6T transceiver order would be completed by the end of this fiscal year, pending product qualification allowing AOI to recognize the full extent of the $200M order backlog in this year alone.

Second, this 1.6T order that AOI won from this hyperscaler meant this hyperscaler customer would re-instate this hyperscaler as a major customer accounting for >10% of AOI’s revenues. In my previous post in March, I explained why there was a high likelihood that Amazon (AMZN) could be the most likely hyperscaler to begin ramping up its AI capex dollars towards AOI’s transceivers and pluggables. Last year, Amazon's AWS revealed that a majority of their data center network was still using 400G solutions, so an upgrade to 800G and 1.6T optical solutions was expected. Plus, Amazon’s AWS also has an active share warrant with AOI, which it might be executing upon, in my view.

The combination of these two implications that I stated leaves enough room for Amazon’s AWS to likely increase the purchases of more transceivers and/or pluggables from AOI. I had explained in my previous post how Amazon’s AWS could easily end up purchasing ~$400M worth of products from AOI, which is greater than the combined ~$200M AWS has already spent on awarding AOI. AOI has called these orders "initial," so there are strong hints that management has left for investors to believe that more orders could be coming.

And that’s what AWS exactly ended up doing by purchasing even more transceivers.

Just over a week ago, AOI announced that “this same customer” now awarded AOI a contract for 800G optical transceivers. The total order was worth $53M, but what was more interesting was the agreed-upon timeline to deliver these 800G transceivers. Following product qualifications, AOI expects to ship the entire batch of 800G transceivers worth $53M by Q3 itself.

The only reason why AOI can commit to such aggressive delivery timelines for its transceivers, especially the 800G transceivers, is because of the company’s vertical integration approach to owning the entire manufacturing capability of its optical transceivers from the ground up. As revealed in their Q4 call with analysts, AOI plans to triple its laser manufacturing capacity at its Texas location, including their production of indium phosphide, an extremely critical component in the laser manufacturing process.

To do so, AOI is rapidly scaling its capex deployment plans to expeditiously ramp production capacity to >500,000 units of 800G and 1.6T transceivers produced per month, up from ~90,000 units of 800G transceivers being currently produced.

Unlike its execution gaps seen in the 2016-17 timeframe, which I noted in my March coverage on AOI, management is already rapidly scaling capex, as demonstrated in Exhibit A below, something I found encouraging amidst the backdrop of recurring backlog wins.

Management has not yet revealed its capex expansion plans for 2026, and this is something I will be watching closely, but for now, I am optimistic about the slate of customer wins that AOI just seems to be racking up for its optical transceivers and pluggables business.

AOI’s Valuation Multiples Are Still Not Reflective of Upside

So far, management has stated that it expects $1B in revenues in CY26. That implies ~119% top-line growth in CY26, but markets are still quite pessimistic, projecting revenues of $962M, 4% lower than what management’s revenue projections are.

Still, if I factor in analysts’ estimates of $962M, I arrive at a forward revenue multiple of 7x. I find this extremely undervalued since I believe AOI could easily fetch a forward revenue multiple of >10x as compared to peers like Lumentum (LITE), which is currently trading at ~18x and 12x forward revenues for ~70% growth per year in FY26 and FY27.

That scope for multiple expansions to 10x implies a minimum upside of ~42% from current levels.

Risks & Other Factors To Note

​​I suspect markets are still skeptical about some of the growth prospects in AOI’s business after witnessing execution gaps and concentration risks from the 2016-2017 time frame. I had explained these risks in detail in my prior posts, and investors must be mindful of the possibility of execution gaps if they invest in AOI. I am watching management’s guidelines closely on how they plan to scale their capex further, since they did not communicate these plans in the Q4 call.

Additionally, investors will note that I had previously highlighted how Microsoft (MSFT) and Oracle (ORCL) were major hyperscaler customers, introducing significant concentration risk. The likely addition of Amazon as a third hyperscaler customer might slightly broaden out the concentration risk.

Finally, I wanted to quickly add that product validation of their transceivers is critical for AOI to ship out the transceiver orders to customers and recognize backlog. In February, AOI announced that they were facing some issues in getting their 800G transceivers validated on Microsoft’s data center network, which resulted in a delay in recognizing ~$4-8M in revenue. While this is normally seen in the industry due to the wide array of accelerators (GPUs, XPUs) being deployed in data centers, investors must watch for such risks as well.

For these reasons, I refrained from upgrading AOI to a Strong Buy rating.

Takeaway

While some risks remain in the rearview mirror, the growing surge in orders being won by Applied Optoelectronics for its optical transceivers and pluggables is making the optical networking company’s outlook a lot stronger than when I last reviewed the company. Recent wins by a third hyperscaler customer suggest AI capex is continuing to increasingly flow towards beneficiaries like Applied Optoelectronics that are in control of the entire manufacturing process for their optical products.

For these reasons, I am not only reiterating my bullish view on Applied Optoelectronics, but also buying a modest position in Applied Optoelectronics.

data.title: The Elmet Group Seeks IPO On Moderate Revenue Growth, Capacity Increase
data.description:
The Elmet Group Is Growing Moderately, Investing In Capacity

The Elmet Group Co. (ELMT) has filed to raise what may reach $115 million in gross proceeds in an IPO, according to an S-1 registration statement.

The company provides a range of critical materials and microwave products primarily for aerospace and defense applications.

ELMT has produced moderate top-line growth and has been investing its operating cash flow in capital improvements to increase capacity; the IPO’s proceeds will be used for deleveraging the balance sheet.

If the IPO is fairly priced and overall stock market volatility is contained, institutional demand should be reasonably strong.

What Does Elmet Group Do?

Elmet Group provides engineered components and high-energy systems for a variety of industries, such as aerospace, defense, government, industrial, semiconductor, electronics, energy and medical industries.

The company is organized under two operating segments, Critical Materials Components [CMC] and Engineered Microwave Products [EMP].

The pie charts below show the revenue contribution for the CMC and EMP segments, and by region, for calendar year 2025:

Its CMC segment primarily produces tungsten and molybdenum products via its U.S.-based manufacturing facilities. These products support numerous critical components involved in U.S. high-performance military and defense equipment.

The firm’s Chairman and CEO since its founding is Peter V. Anania, who is also President of precursor firm Anania & Associates and has been a founder, President or CEO of more than 20 manufacturing companies. (Source - SEC.)

Elmet has received approximately $1.5 million in fair market value equity investment to-date from various investors, including Mr. Anania, and had outstanding debt of $53.5 million as of the end of 2025.

What Are Elmet’s Markets?

Elmet operates in multiple markets, including critical materials and engineered high-power microwave products.

The global tungsten market was an estimated $1.9 billion in 2024 and was projected to grow to $2.8 billion by 2033, according to a market research report by Grand View Research.

If reached, this growth rate would be a CAGR of 4.7% from 2025 to 2033, a moderate estimate.

The Asia Pacific market represented the highest market share in 2024, at 54%, while the U.S. tungsten market is expected to increase by a CAGR of 3.9% through 2033, with the automotive, aerospace and defense sectors accounting for significant demand growth.

The chart below shows the historical and projected future growth trajectory of the industry through 2033, by form:

Competitive market participants include:

Critical Materials:

  • Plansee SE - Austria.

  • Small resellers of PRC products.

Engineered Microwave Products:

  • AFT GmbH - Germany.

  • Spinner GmbH - Germany.

  • Microtech - U.S.

  • Dielectric LLC.

  • S-Team s r.o. (Slovakia).

  • Amtek - U.S.

  • Sairem - France.

The U.S. tungsten market is seeing robust growth, driven primarily by national security concerns and supply chain vulnerabilities.

While the U.S. has historically relied upon China for its tungsten needs, the Biden administration placed a 26% tariff on certain Chinese tungsten products as part of a broader package that targeted critical supply chain inputs.

For its secondary market, the company is also active to a lesser degree in the Engineered Microwave Products market, which Fortune Business Insights said was nearly $7.5 billion in 2025 and projected it to grow to almost $14 billion by 2034, in its 2026 market research report.

That market is forecasted to grow at a CAGR of 7.15% from 2026 to 2034, with substantial demand coming from telecommunications infrastructure, defense and space sectors and healthcare devices.

The firm’s strengths include its U.S.-based positioning for mission-critical products, vertical integration, and engineering expertise and focus on early design involvement to better embed the company into client programs.

Weaknesses the company faces are the capital-intensive nature of its work, complex operations and exposure to government contracts, which can result in "lumpy revenue" and binary outcomes.

Opportunities for the firm are a trend toward defense modernization and production base increases, reshoring of production away from foreign providers to U.S.-based suppliers and expansion into nuclear, industrial electrification and space applications.

Threats to management’s goals could include raw material inflation or supply disruption, defense program delays or cancellations, or exposure to fixed-price contracts as the Department of Defense increasingly shifts cost risk to suppliers.

The Elmet Group’s Recent Financial Results

The company's recent financial results show moderately growing top-line revenue, but gross margin has fallen by 80 basis points, operating margin has dropped by 150 basis points, net margin was lower by 540 basis points, and cash flow from operations was cut by more than half, as the table shows here:

Total Revenue

(Source - SEC.)

Sales & Marketing costs as a percentage of revenue have been stable and low, per the table here:

Sales and Marketing

(Source - SEC.)

Its Sales & Marketing efficiency rate was a reasonably solid 1.7x in the most recent reporting period, and its core operating leverage ratio was flat, at a weak 0.24x.

Elmet’s IPO Plan

ELMT plans to sell shares of its common stock in an IPO that may exceed $115 million, excluding normal (15%) share allotments to underwriters.

Shareholder sale lock-ups will be for the typical 180-day period after the effective date of the registration statement, as filed by the SEC.

Management plans to use the net proceeds of the IPO for:

The principal purposes of this offering are to obtain additional capital to support our operations, to create a public market for our common stock and to facilitate our future access to the public equity markets. We currently intend that the net proceeds received by us from this offering will be used in part for repayment of certain debts, including [i] the Great Falls Term Loan (as defined below) and [ii] the President Line of Credit (as defined below) and [iii] pre-Reorganization intercompany debt owed to AAI, as described below, with the remainder of the proceeds to be put toward working capital, growth capital (as discussed below), and general corporate purposes.

(Source - SEC.)

The company's online roadshow presentation is currently not available, although by SEC regulation, it may not include any information not included in the latest registration statement, so it’s a bit repetitive in that regard.

Management said that the company is not subject to any legal proceedings that it believes would have a material adverse effect on its financial condition or operations.

Underwriters for the IPO include Cantor, Needham & Company, Canaccord Genuity and Roth Capital Partners.

Capital And Liquidity Dynamics

At the end of 2025, Elmet had $2 million in cash and equivalents and $115 million in total liabilities, of which $53.5 million was debt.

For the calendar year 2025, the firm generated free cash flow of only $305,000 on $10.4 million in capex against $10.7 million in operating cash flow, so the company has been a capital-intensive operation, at least in 2025.

Working capital was nearly $50 million with a current ratio of 1.83x, so balance sheet coverage is fine, but a very low absolute cash balance indicates tight liquidity that is tied up in receivables and inventory instead of cash.

So, effectively, all of the operating cash flow has been reinvested into the firm’s asset base, which exposes the company if margins fall.

Also, the firm’s debt is primarily floating-rate, so the company may be sensitive to higher rates if inflation picks up in 2026 due to external shocks (like oil prices).

With around $53 million in debt and minimal cash on hand, the IPO will largely be a deleveraging event, shoring up the balance sheet, but not providing the resources to materially accelerate growth.

Elmet Group Is Growing Moderately In A Positive Environment

ELMT has produced moderate top-line revenue growth and gross profit growth, but net profit has declined, and operating cash flow has dropped as well.

Management has been focusing on increasing capacity, investing in specialized assets and various growth projects, so the numbers don’t look as favorable as they might in the run-up to the IPO.

To that end, the company's growth strategy entails producing more output from its existing installed base, expanding through customer-driven product development and increasing its exposure to defense, energy and research programs.

The firm also has a significant acquisition history, and leadership plans to continue with the addition of tuck-in deals to add market scope, technology and customer access.

Elmet operates in multiple markets, critical materials and high-energy microwave products, all of which indicate a moderate growth trajectory in the coming years, although a recent emphasis by the current U.S. administration on reshoring and increasing production of defense products could increase the demand curve substantially over time.

Elmet’s non-China sourcing ability positions it well for the current environment that favors domestic producers for national security and skill reshoring purposes.

The company presents risks to investors, including its reliance on government contracts, which can make for big contract wins or losses, leading to an unpredictable revenue trajectory.

Also, Elmet is a "smaller reporting company" and "emerging growth company," which allows management to produce less information for shareholders, a negative in the public markets, as many of these kinds of company stocks have disappointed investors.

Given the company's levered balance sheet, the IPO proceeds will be used to pay down debt instead of for future growth and development initiatives, a negative for new shareholders.

While the firm has invested in its ability to scale, it remains labor-constrained for its specialized products.

And, since the company operates two segments, it will be difficult to find a direct public comparable for valuation purposes, increasing the uncertainty of whether the IPO will be reasonably priced or overpriced.

With a significant portion of its revenue derived from U.S. government contracts, Elmet has meaningful customer concentration risk.

Also, $17.3 million of its debt is related-party debt, out of $53.5 million in total debt, so the firm has material related-party transactions in its history.

Overall, Elmet appears to be a moderate-growth, strategically important niche defense vendor, but with weakening financial quality and low core operating leverage despite increased production capacity which holds the prospect of operating profit growth.

Institutional investors may be receptive if management's pricing and valuation assumptions are reasonable and overall stock market volatility is reduced.

Expected IPO Pricing Date: To be determined.

data.title: CorMedix: Waiting On Phase III ReSPECT For A Further Re-Rating (Upgrade)
data.description:
Thesis

CorMedix Inc. (CRMD) stock has been on the downward slope since early January. Shares hit a mid-$6 low in late March and have seen some slight upside into the $7 range. In my previous coverage, I initiated my Sell rating due to CMS reimbursement changes impacting DefenCath pricing. However, I do see some upside coming from the Phase III ReSPECT readout sometime in Q2 '26. In the longer run, this could unlock a $2 billion prophylaxis market for Rezzayo and shift the company toward a sort of higher-margin, speciality hospital model. In the near term, however, the trial readout should serve as a meaningful catalyst, which is why I am choosing to upgrade to a Hold rating until we see that readout.

FY25 review

Just as a quick recap on earnings. For Q4 '25, the company reported net revenue of about $128.6 million, which was a big jump year-over-year from the $31.2 million we saw back in Q4 '24. We can put the big driver down to about $91.2 million in DefenCath sales and $37.4 million from Melinta’s portfolio. So I think we can say that there’s been a successful market penetration and adoption among outpatient dialysis customers for DefenCath. The Melinta acquisition, on the other hand, contributed a full quarter of revenue for the first time, which was nice to see. However, they missed on EPS expectations with just $0.16 per diluted share, but they also managed to achieve net income of $14.0 million. It’s worth noting that this was supported by deferred tax assets that offset a bit of the tax expense. Adjusted EBITDA actually surged to $77.2 million compared to $15.3 million last year, so for me, this is a sign of decent underlying profitability and efficient cost management, even if we are seeing increased operational expenses.

As for FY25, CorMedix’s total revenue hit $311.7 million, with pro forma revenue, which would include a full year of Melinta operations, coming to about $401.3 million. So there’s clearly a significant contribution of the acquisition to overall growth here. Elsewhere, net income managed to reach to $163.1 million, or about $2.04 per diluted share, compared with a net loss of $17.9 million back in 2024. Again, this is down to both revenue growth and their use of those deferred tax assets. In terms of spending, operating expenses more than doubled year-over-year to $125.6 million, again from the Melinta acquisition, but also from the integration costs and stock-based compensation we saw throughout the year. They’re also continuing to invest in research and development, particularly for expanded indications of DefenCath.

Coming to liquidity, things are still quite strong. They hold about $148.5 million in cash and short-term investments, which should support ongoing clinical programs and the newly announced $75 million share repurchase initiative. In terms of guidance, CorMedix maintains 2026 guidance of $300 to $320 million in net revenue and $100 to $125 million in adjusted EBITDA. We also have some upcoming clinical milestones, including Phase 3 results for Rezzayo prophylaxis, which could potentially drive some much-needed growth.

Current pipeline catalysts

Now, CorMedix’s clinical pipeline is mainly focused on two key programs, both of which could meaningfully expand its commercial footprint beyond just DefenCath’s current hemodialysis indication. The most advanced candidate would be Rezzayo/rezafungin, which is a once‑weekly echinocandin antifungal that is already approved in the U.S. for treating both candidemia and invasive candidiasis. However, the next step for Rezzayo would be an expansion into prophylaxis of invasive fungal infections for adults undergoing allogeneic blood and marrow transplant.

It’s a rather tight but quite meaningful niche with the current evaluation being done in the global Phase III ReSPECT study. Enrollment for this trial is already complete, with top‑line data expected sometime in Q2 '26. So, this would make it a big near-term catalyst. The aim here would be expanded indication and drive growth in a pretty high‑unmet‑need population.

Other than Rezzayo, CorMedix is also looking to advance DefenCath into a few new clinical settings. So, beyond just its established use to prevent catheter‑related bloodstream infections in adult hemodialysis patients, the company has now initiated a Phase III study in total parenteral nutrition patients. That would then target the prevention of central line‑associated bloodstream infection. We can expect a completion sometime in H1 '27.

Rezzayo expansion

As you know, the Phase III ReSPECT trial is going to be quite pivotal for CorMedix’s share price recovery, so it’s worth taking a quick look at what kind of results we need to see. It’s a global, randomized, double-blind study designed to evaluate Rezzayo as a prophylactic antifungal therapy in high-risk allogeneic blood and marrow transplant (BMT) patients. Now, in simple terms, that means Rezzayo being used as a prophylactic antifungal therapy, so giving the drug before a fungal infection happens, rather than waiting to treat one after it appears. Specifically, it would be for patients undergoing bone marrow transplants since it’s usually the case that they have extremely weak immune systems. It’s high risk since even a small fungal exposure can turn into a serious, life-threatening infection.

So prophylaxis is like a preventive shield against this, with the main goal being to stop infections from developing in the first place. As a brief example, imagine a patient who just received a bone marrow transplant. Their immune system is going to be temporarily wiped out, so they’re now at high risk of infections from fungi that are usually harmless to us. In the current setting, a doctor would typically give daily antifungal pills such as azoles to try to prevent infection. But this is where Rezzayo can offer a bit of differentiation. You see, instead of taking medication every day, the patient could simply receive a once-weekly IV dose that stays in the body at effective levels the entire time. Which would continuously protect them during that high-risk period.

The key difference here from the currently approved use is down to both timing and purpose. Right now, Rezzayo is approved to treat active fungal infections like candidemia. So that means it’s used after a patient is already infected. But in the prophylaxis setting, it would be used much earlier, before infection occurs, to prevent it entirely. It’s a big shift, but also very important because prevention in these patients can reduce hospitalizations significantly. This could be particularly true considering the drug is easier to use and has fewer drug interactions than the current daily therapies.

Mechanistically speaking, rezafungin would be a next-generation echinocandin which can inhibit β-1,3-D-glucan synthesis. Now, that synthesis is a pretty essential component of the fungal cell wall, so by stopping it, it can then lead to cell lysis and death. It’s also worth mentioning that what differentiates rezafungin, and makes it particularly well-suited for prophylaxis in my opinion, is its exceptionally long half-life, which we see at around 130 plus hours. That’s what allows for a once-weekly dosing with decent sustained drug exposure. Going forward, this is a major advantage in transplant patients, who we typically see needing complex, multi-drug daily prophylaxis regimens. These are often limited by toxicity, drug-drug interactions, especially with regard to chemotherapy, and also some adherence challenges. So, in having a single-agent, long-acting antifungal that covers things like Candida and Aspergillus, this overall process could be simplified a lot.

Phase III ReSPECT trial

As for the trial design, ReSPECT is structured as a head-to-head comparison against the standard antimicrobial prophylaxis, as you would expect. The bull case here is that we see Rezzayo outperform the competition, which typically consists of azoles like fluconazole or posaconazole.

Patients are being randomized to receive either once-weekly IV rezafungin at a 400 mg loading dose followed by 200 mg weekly for about 13 weeks or the multi-drug standard regimen. The primary endpoint is set as fungal-free survival at Day 90, which is actually a composite endpoint that is set to capture both the prevention of invasive fungal disease (IFD) and survival. In terms of a clinically/regulatorily meaningful outcome, I do see a lot of upside here if results are positive. And by a positive outcome, I mean the trial must at minimum demonstrate non-inferiority here, with superiority being ideal. We also need to see some favorable secondary outcomes, such as reduced incidence of IFD and lower discontinuation due to toxicity.

As for the all-cause mortality, it should be comparable. Just to mention the FDA perspective here, I think success would require not just statistical non-inferiority in fungal-free survival, but also a nice, clean safety profile and evidence of some clinical advantages. The main advantages I see the FDA focusing on would be fewer adverse events or meaningful reductions in drug-drug interactions. This is especially true when we consider that prophylaxis is administered to vulnerable but not yet infected patients. As for a timeline, we can expect the data readout in Q2 '26, and if results are positive, I’d expect CorMedix to then submit a supplemental NDA to the FDA thereafter. So the commercial opportunity is not so much the short-term catalyst but rather a positive data readout.

A word on the TAM

Just to touch briefly on the TAM for Rezzayo in the expanded prophylaxis setting for allogeneic bone marrow transplant patients, I see it as a pretty clearly defined and commercially meaningful opportunity. The big driver here would be the concentrated but high-value patient population. CorMedix is estimating about 130,000 eligible patients annually in the U.S., which should translate into an implied TAM of over $2 billion for this indication alone. It's worth noting that there is a very high per-patient value here because of the severity of the condition. Now, these would be critically immunocompromised patients where preventing IFDs could significantly reduce mortality and ICU stays. What's important to consider is that prophylaxis is not a one-time dose but a multi-week regimen over about 13 weeks in the ReSPECT trial, which means each patient would then represent a full course of therapy rather than a single treatment event.

As for pricing, it’s still rather early to be considering what price Rezzayo would likely see, but it would be positioned as a premium hospital-administered therapy. That being said, it would still be anchored to existing antifungal standards of care. Current echinocandins and azole-based prophylaxis regimens can cost well into several thousand dollars per treatment course, and given Rezzayo’s differentiated profile of a once-weekly dosing, reduced drug-drug interactions, and its overall broad pathogen coverage, I’d expect it could reasonably command a total course price in the $15,000 to $25,000 range per patient in the U.S.

This would also align with the implied TAM math at about 130,000 patients, giving us a roughly $2 billion market. Now, the driver of pricing power here will not just be efficacy, but rather pharmacoeconomic benefit. In the sense that if Rezzayo can demonstrate fewer hospitalizations and lower toxicity, payers may accept a premium price since there’s the added benefit of a simpler dosing regimen. So I see the market less about volume expansion and more about value capture per high-risk patient here. With a potential set-up being Rezzayo as a high-margin, specialty hospital drug with strong reimbursement support if that Phase III data is compelling.

Valuation

CorMedix is currently trading at a market cap of about $536.8 million with an enterprise value nearing $526.8 million. They also have a pretty balanced capital structure with $148.5 million in cash and $148.9 million in total debt.

So, from a valuation standpoint, the stock would seem to be pretty attractively priced relative to both historical and sector benchmarks. The forward price-to-earnings ratio on a non-GAAP basis sits at 8.43x, which is well below the Health Care sector median of 17.22x. So that tells me the market is heavily discounting their future earnings potential despite the FY25 net income we saw of $163.1 million and pro forma revenue of $401.3 million. Elsewhere, the EV to EBITDA ratio of 4.38x on a forward basis is a lot lower than the sector median of 11.28x, which again would imply that the market is undervaluing the company’s operational profitability and cash flow generation.

I think we can put this discount mainly down to investor caution around execution risks related to their ongoing clinical programs. A good deal of caution would also be priced in around the integration of the Melinta acquisition, and the variability of future revenue growth, especially from DefenCath and Rezzayo. So overall, the market seems to be pricing CorMedix more as a lower-risk, underappreciated growth play, where the current multiples could potentially imply modest revenue expansion, but there are still some conservative assumptions about successful Phase 3 clinical outcomes.

Looking ahead

So just to conclude here, CorMedix is a stronger business than its current valuation suggests. There was solid FY25 profitability and meaningful revenue scale following the Melinta acquisition on last earnings. So this changes the picture somewhat going into Q2 '26. However, the investment case ultimately hinges on execution here, both commercially with DefenCath and clinically with Rezzayo. The upcoming Phase III ReSPECT data in Q2 '26 is the next defining catalyst where I see the upside coming from. There's a potential to unlock a sizable, high-margin prophylaxis market and materially shift future investor sentiment.

So, to restate my thesis, CorMedix is a discounted, catalyst-driven opportunity, in which the market is pricing in execution risk and conservative growth assumptions. If we do see positive Phase III Rezzayo data, this is clear potential for a valuation re-rating. But the downside still remains from the clinical uncertainty and near-term commercial pressures. Which is why I am now choosing to upgrade my CRMD rating to a Hold.
